Just Plain Mat 04/10/2025 19:01

1
Michael Yang 04/10/2025 19:36
I wonder why people value the covered flip so much. What's the ideal use case you're imagining here that doesn't involve <psychic 1>?
Michael Yang
I wonder why people value the covered flip so much. What's the ideal use case you're imagining here that doesn't involve <psychic 1>?
CompileBot BOT 04/10/2025 19:36
CompileBot v1.0.2
Funny Samy 04/10/2025 19:42
I assumed psychic 1 was boosting that vote lol
Michael Yang 04/10/2025 19:43
As it should But I am curious about other use cases
klenz 04/10/2025 19:55
reasons for me:
  • there usually are covered cards on the board, either your own side or the opponents side, so this action will nearly always be useful
  • just to get a benefit of the points here can already help, imagine flipping opponents 5 face-down to a 2, lots of value just in the numbers
  • and the interaction opportunity with covered cards is very rare in the game, there are lots of other cards that allow you to draw cards, shift cards and discard opponents cards
 (edited)
19:57
  • and finally the power moves, disable opponents card with active top ability by flipping them, or maybe enable one of your own, currently face-down cards again - what a massive shift in game state
 (edited)
Just Plain Mat 04/10/2025 22:07
Agree on those points. It was by a small margin that I didn't vote for that over the Draw 3 cards
Eris 04/10/2025 23:38
It was a tough choice. I haven't played main 2, but tho these points are true, I feel the potential benefit of shifting is really high. Point 1 still stands for shifting and the ability to shift a card only limited by it being uncovered is also rare and versatile. Retriggering certain key middle commands, rearranging to compile or gain control or forcing your opponent to compile can decide the game in your favor.
Reynard 05/10/2025 0:20
More or less what klenz said. I originally wasn't thinking flip would be good, then I realized it deals with cards that are usually protected and that can make some big swings (5s and 6s on your side or the opponent's, annoying top text, etc). Shifting is a feel good mechanic for me because I enjoy chain reactions, but sometimes the chain reaction is a trap option and I'd be better off doing something simpler.
0:22
Draw and discard are both action economy plays, which is very powerful but deceptively situational. I've definitely held on to draw and discard effects longer than shift or flip effects on average because I didn't want to overdraw or leave my opponent with easy choices.
0:22
(though I do like discard 2 for the ability to mess up a game plan on top of action economy)
Prateek 05/10/2025 0:33
There is a case for random discard in the end game
Bombz 05/10/2025 23:26
Even if flipping a covered card isn't necessarily the biggest impact on board, it still seems like the most powerful effect to me. The default rules for effects protect covered cards, so there's a lot of power in the ability to bypass that. Simply having that ability in your deck (or knowing your opponent does) will change the way the whole match is played. (edited)
Michael Yang 09/10/2025 22:04
poll_question_text
Which of these commands would you consider to be the most generally powerful?

10:47
As the GTG guys put it in the streams, "with control is the way to play compile". (edited)

Reynard 15/10/2025 13:44
I'm guessing that the kind of audience that you'd get on the discord will bias the results a bit but I do feel like the game is substantially missing somethig without control
Eris 15/10/2025 13:48
100% agree. Also, I don't really know, but it feels like playing without it must make comebacks really difficult and the game less interesting when someone gets to the second compile.
klenz 15/10/2025 13:50
the GTG guys said that if control is not used, the game just comes down to "rush strategies". i think they are right.
Eris 15/10/2025 13:55
Exactly, the moment you get to your second compile you can just rush for the third (even just playing face down cards) and your opponent will run out of things to counter it. Also all your other 5's and more cards will become kinda useless
Reynard
I'm guessing that the kind of audience that you'd get on the discord will bias the results a bit but I do feel like the game is substantially missing somethig without control
NZ3R0K 15/10/2025 13:57
Control is a large component, but can be left out for new players until they are ready - as suggested in the rulebook. Taught a friend to play the other day - did 3 games in 1 session. 13:57
game feels weird without it though I will say...

I'd play a game or two without control if I was introducing someone to the game but I feel like some of the protocols make much less sense without it

Michael Yang
I wonder why people value the covered flip so much. What's the ideal use case you're imagining here that doesn't involve <psychic 1>?
BlueMoons 15/10/2025 18:44
if it's any covered card? Well there's more covered options to pick from than uncovered cards usually. a line can only have 2 uncovered cards but maybe like 5 covered cards to get to 10 points you need at least 1 covered card in the line, and the fewer there are the bigger the point swing is. you can also prevent a card from retriggering with this. Of the listed effects in the poll this is the only one that is basically guaranteed to change point totals. (edited)
klenz
reasons for me:
  • there usually are covered cards on the board, either your own side or the opponents side, so this action will nearly always be useful
  • just to get a benefit of the points here can already help, imagine flipping opponents 5 face-down to a 2, lots of value just in the numbers
  • and the interaction opportunity with covered cards is very rare in the game, there are lots of other cards that allow you to draw cards, shift cards and discard opponents cards
 (edited)
BlueMoons 15/10/2025 18:47
basically everything you said lol

Atomik 16/10/2025 10:07
If you had to name just one 3 protocol combo as the easiest to win with when it comes to new player games which would it be?
klenz 16/10/2025 10:23
Just from Main 1, which is the ideal starting point, the best "beginner" protocols probably are: GRAVITY, LIFE, LIGHT, SPIRIT and WATER
10:23
in no particular order
10:24
you could also add SPEED to that, and you have 6 protocols to mix and match an have a couple of games with
Michael Yang 16/10/2025 17:30
I like Light, Psychic, Plague for newbies. The discard synergy is easy to use and feels satisfying, plenty of draw so new players don't have to think about refreshing as much, and Light gives a variety of interesting interaction pieces. It's a straightforward, aggressive deck where you don't have to worry about timing windows as much.
klenz
Just from Main 1, which is the ideal starting point, the best "beginner" protocols probably are: GRAVITY, LIFE, LIGHT, SPIRIT and WATER
Funny Samy 16/10/2025 17:44
Gonna more or less second this, I think water + life is straightforward to extract value from
Reynard 16/10/2025 18:02
I feel like that depends on the player. Water and life have good easy value but they also ask you to keep more things in your head to get there. Life 0 is a bit of a timing puzzle, water 4 has a lot of trap options in how it can be played and used, life 3 and 4 care about where they are in a stack, etc
18:04
I know people that'd be the perfect hook for, but if I'm introducing someone random to the game I'd hew closer to stuff love psychic where every card works basically every time
TheBigBoy 16/10/2025 18:05
I like giving newer players fire + lots of draw because it lets them discard stuff they can’t figure out how to use

4's are situational. it's fine. you can redistribute some points or force your opponent to compile. 4's aren't supposed to be slam dunk plays all the time.
Swordodo 30/10/2025 20:09
poll_question_text
Creating some custom protocols at the moment. What do you think is the best effect for a card of value 4?
victor_answer_votes
5
total_votes
11
victor_answer_id
1
victor_answer_text
Shift this card to the line that matches it’s protocol
SleepyPanda27 30/10/2025 21:27
4s being situation is interesting though, because they aren't situational in the sense that some situations they are good, but most they are fine, it feels more like some situations they are good, and a lot of them they are actively bad
21:27
Like I think I would prefer a 4 with no text in many cases
21:28
Which I think is just interesting when a lot of 3s are situationally quite good but flatly okay as well
21:31
I also don't think this is a complaint about the design btw, more a discussion of strategy (edited)
21:31
I think its pretty interesting design decision and is not something that is even actually true across the board
21:32
But I think if you look at Main 1 + Aux 1, I find myself discarding 4s I think more than any number, and also feel lile there are many game states where my 4s feel actively game losing to play face up
21:37
I think in general I wish that most 4s had a "you may" clause associated with them
21:39
Like in the world where 5s are "slight downside for 5 power" and 3s are in general "upside for 3 power" it feels like 4s ought to be "on average neutral/slight upside for 4 power", like light 4 feels like that curve holds true to a tee, but many others in main 1 + aux 1 don't
xcyborgninja 31/10/2025 6:17
Maybe I haven't played enough to see all the downsides everyone else sees. And I honestly haven't thought much about opponents retriggering my cards to hurt me much so I'll think on that. But I feel like most 4s are actually pretty useful but take planning ahead a turn or so to make them useful. Darkness and Gravity 4 can be a response to your card being flipped or you can play a card face down on purpose. Then it actually adds 6 points to your line, protects itself and can retrigger whatever you just uncovered. Death 4 is a great counter to a pesky opponent card to clear its effects, which is handy because there are a lot of low cards with strong effects. Fire 4 gives you the strongest deck churn ability I think in Main 1, meaning if your hand is full of situational cards that aren't useful you can try to draw better ones, especially if you're trying to pull out a specific card to make a strong play. Life 4 is nearly a free draw 1 card, just don't play it as the first card in a stack. Also safe to retrigger because there's no down side. Light 4 I honestly don't use much but knowing the cards you need to watch out for in the near future (looking at you Death 2 and Gravity 0) can let you counter attack or give up on a battle you know you're about to lose. Plague 4 has already been discussed above so I won't repeat it. Psychic 4 is maybe weaker, but if you leave it uncovered it can give you a reaction you redeem later to "skip" your opponent's last turn. This could delay a compile while you do something else with your main action.
SleepyPanda27
Like I think I would prefer a 4 with no text in many cases
Eris 31/10/2025 8:56
What 4s do you feel are actively bad apart from Hate 4 and Water 4?
SleepyPanda27
4s being situation is interesting though, because they aren't situational in the sense that some situations they are good, but most they are fine, it feels more like some situations they are good, and a lot of them they are actively bad
BlueMoons 31/10/2025 9:30
4 is double the amount of points you get unconditionally from playing a face-down. 4 points are always going to be relevant at some point in the game, but not every turn is a good time to play them.
Eris
What 4s do you feel are actively bad apart from Hate 4 and Water 4?
SleepyPanda27 31/10/2025 17:03
I didn't mean all are actively bad (in fact a lot of 4s are actually really strong in their ideal cases), but death 4, speed 4, and gravity 4 can all end up being quite bad depending on board state, in the sense that a textless 4 power would be better than playing the 4 with the text (edited)
17:04
Of course they can also end up being stronger too
17:04
I just find that 4s feel bricked more often than most other cards
17:07
Apathy 4 can feel awkward to play
Mirrorcat 31/10/2025 17:09
Apathy in general feels a bit awkward depending on matchup, in my experience
17:09
Which is kind of thematic tbh
SleepyPanda27 31/10/2025 17:09
I suppose that 4s feel like they tend to have a design of "there needs to be some amount of set up for me to be good", even the pretty flatly strong Life 4 still wants you to have a turn prepping for it, but I think the requirement of 4s needing prep to be "strong" often makes them feel less flexible, and instances where I want to forfeit the value from a 4 and just get the flat 4 power, I often feel locked out of those plays (edited)
SleepyPanda27
Apathy 4 can feel awkward to play
Eris 31/10/2025 17:10

Eris 31/10/2025 17:14
Yeah, the only difference is that you can use it to cover a bottom text or the super rare case the opponent doesn't have cards on their deck. I also think that sometimes in this game the value of the number changes depending on context
17:16
Playing Gravity 6 in a line where you have 4 and the opponent has 0 has much more value than playing it in a contested line. Even if the numerical difference is the same, how fast it gets you to 10 matters more (edited)

incorrectname 02/11/2025 5:30
Consider a deck with blank cards and only numbers, what numbers would be balanced? e.g blank 3,3,3,4,4,4 or something I assume this would be related to whatever calculations were made when designing the decks like some points/turn value of a deck
Funny Samy 02/11/2025 5:40
luck synergy!!!
incorrectname 05/11/2025 18:32
no thoughts
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 05/11/2025 18:38
Hm, its a bit of a strange question, but my guess is that since 4s tend to be the most "neutral" cards in terms of effects, it would just be a deck of all 4s. That'd mean it'd take 3 cards to Compile a line, which seems about average. My guess is that'd be the most vanilla deck, but I have no idea how the underlying math works with the other protocols !
Just Plain Mat 05/11/2025 19:22
I was initially thinking that there would still need some slight variety, not just all of 1 number. But I think that analysis of 4s being the most "neutral" would be correct although they usually do have some incentive to be played. But then incentive does give them additional "value" beyond the printed 4 value. So with that I was thinking that cards would on average need to have 4.5 in value But then I started thinking of the 5s. They are the ones that require a cost which would mean that the printed 5 value is too high in comparison with others. But that's likely only because of the existence of the other cards. Without the 5 card in a deck you would need all 4 (1+2+3+4) positive valued cards of the Protocol to reach value 10.
19:23
I just ran through it statistically to also see what that would lead to. That saying if you had all 6 cards with only their values and no effects, there's 20 out of 64 combinations to get to or above the 10 value. Roughly speaking that could mean the average value of a card is 20/64 or 3.125
Eris 05/11/2025 19:24
I thought the same with the 4.5 thing
19:29
That said, all 4s would be the same as all 4s but one 5, you still always need 3 cards to compile. If you add another 5, then you can sometimes compile with 2 cards. So maybe how many 5s you'll had would depend on the desired chances of that happening.
19:30
Because I don't think it would make sense to say it would be all 4s looking at all the exsiting 4s in the game
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 05/11/2025 19:36
No yeah I could see the 4.5 thing. So maybe it'd be all blank (4,4,4,5,5,5)? Cause that adds up to 27, which is the same as (4.5 * 6). And I guess technically as long as the numbers add up to 27 its fine, but a blank 3 or 2 would be a strange thing to have in an already strange deck
Just Plain Mat 05/11/2025 19:42
A blank 2 would definitely be too low but having something like one 3, four 4s, and one 5 may help make it work. Or some kind of offset that would still get up to that 27 value or whatever is needed. That way there is still the strategy of using the lower value 3 card to help keep hold of the 4 or 5 values for when it's better to have that value and not lose it during compile
incorrectname 05/11/2025 19:48
the question was basically a way to gauge the value of cards in other decks since for each deck by themselves there is a threshold of points per turn that they cannot keep up with; of course interaction matters a lot though. for the deck with only blanks things are also a bit worse given that you are playing a card per turn rather which is more easily dealt with compared to other ways of producing points per turn (imagine playing down 5 blank 1s down a column)

16:09
I especially like: 1 (nets you a card, blocks enemy compiles for a turn so they commit somewhere or refresh, either way you gain time, information and options) (edited)
16:10

16:12
6 (is a 6, demands answers or straight up compiles a line fast)

SleepyPanda27
Like in the world where 5s are "slight downside for 5 power" and 3s are in general "upside for 3 power" it feels like 4s ought to be "on average neutral/slight upside for 4 power", like light 4 feels like that curve holds true to a tee, but many others in main 1 + aux 1 don't
opda2056 08/11/2025 13:30
Not to rehash a discussion but I actually see 5 text as upside, often. Due to how the game works, discard is often delayed cycling, and I find that being down on cards and then having to refresh, or worse, compile refresh, can put you SUPER far behind when your opponent gains a ton of tempo. 5s let you control stronger refresh times, and discard your more situationally good cards for tempo swings
Eris 08/11/2025 16:23
I find that a lot of the times I'm refreshing with two cards for the reason you said, it's really important to time your refreshes because you are exposed the turns you are playing your last cards. It's true that, in this situations where you don't feel like your cards are worth the risk of waiting until 0-1 to refresh, a 5 doesn't really have much of a negative impact and even makes you draw one more card.
16:25
But maybe the intent of 5 making you discard was for them to have a downside? So I still think maybe in the last discussion it still makes sense that the "average flat value" would be between 4 and 5? idk
opda2056 08/11/2025 22:21
Yeah it’s definitely a downside in terms of card quantity, and they have weird timings to play around. If you have a bunch of good cards in hand, they hasten the turns you need to refresh by one, or they are the last card you play. If you draw two of them, it gets a little more complex too, but it also adds 5 power to the board, which is big tempo haha
22:22
I played a Peace Clarity Corruption deck with my only game of Main 2 so far, and the downside became a huge upside on the 5s. I thought it was super strong
Funny Samy 09/11/2025 0:44
I think that's more a peace thing than anything lol
0:45
i definitely experienced something similar with peace where there was incentive to route 5s into the play line to cull handsize
Michael Yang 10/11/2025 21:00
People always ask me about changing the 5's and it's always easy for me to disagree - I really love where they stand. And it's a cool bit of protocol identity to think about which 5's are "better" than others. I agree that Peace 5 is a good example of a strong 5. While Fire 5 is one that immediately comes to mind as a weak one. Then I think there's lots of room for interpretation. For example, I think Psychic 5 is also a strong one.
Funny Samy 10/11/2025 21:07
I like that the 5s all have the same text, it relieves some of the mental load if you already know a sixth of what each deck has, and it kind of unifies the game's design in a way
BlueMoons 10/11/2025 21:59
5 - discard 1 card is solid in most play patterns
21:59
it means that every deck has at least a couple cards with negative middle commands that you can trigger
21:59
it's got an easy optimisation way of avoiding the downside (play it as your last card)
22:01
having some commonality with all protocols is nice
22:01
i do think that from a drafting perspective it would be fun to switch it up at some point
22:02
because after maybe 4-6 main sets you've kind of seen every context of 5 - discard 1 card
22:02
and then you could look at 5 - delete 1 of your cards or 5 - return 1 card to the bottom of your deck as the 5 for every protocol in the set and see how that changes the vibe of the set (edited)

it means that every deck has at least a couple cards with negative middle commands that you can trigger

Michael Yang
People always ask me about changing the 5's and it's always easy for me to disagree - I really love where they stand. And it's a cool bit of protocol identity to think about which 5's are "better" than others. I agree that Peace 5 is a good example of a strong 5. While Fire 5 is one that immediately comes to mind as a weak one. Then I think there's lots of room for interpretation. For example, I think Psychic 5 is also a strong one.
Eris 13/11/2025 8:42
I'm curious, why is Psychic 5 strong? It's because the other Psychic cards already give you an action economy advantage so the downside is less noticible?
Michael Yang 13/11/2025 16:01
Pretty much. Psychic has a lot of draw already but you'll still have to refresh. So the 5 helps cycle effectively. Psychic's low value cards are also focused on manipulation instead of board presence so the 5 plays an important power role.
Funny Samy
What would you guys say the general gameplan of the Corruption protocol is? Like what is it trying to do
klenz 13/11/2025 16:04

I am not saying you cannot compile with corruption, just that its focus is more geared towards corrupting (flipping facedowm) your opponents field and hand. rapanui 30/11/2025 19:51
For Compile 1, I've played now enough times that I'm starting to get a sense of the baseline tiers of the protocols. Extremely First-Pickable: Death, Darkness, Plague, Psychic, Fire. Happy To End Up With It: Speed, Life, Water. Can Be Good with the Right Friends: Gravity, Light, Spirit How Do I Work With This?: Metal I'd like to hear people's takes.
Cambru 30/11/2025 20:02
I think its wild. Gravity is one of my favourites, and speed has some crazy wombo potential. These two absolutely first pickable in my eyes. Metal can be tough though, agree on that one
20:03
The flexibility of spirit cant be understated - is also one of my faves i think
rapanui 30/11/2025 20:10
Yes, a mental shortcut I've kind of settled on a bit is that it really helps to have a protocol that can mess with your opponent's stuff directly. 'Delete' as a verb is less situational than Flip or Shift. Flip and Shift can sometimes get you value, (by, say, uncovering a 5 to force a discard). Delete is only situationally bad if Deleting a thing uncovers an effect you'd rather not lose. BUT, you ALSO want protocols that can do cute 'value plays' on your side of the board. Like re-proccing Psychic 0 over and over is so gross. So, let's say you have to play a game of Compile against the literal Grim Reaper, and s/he being Very Kind, allows you to choose any 3 protocols for yourself AND for them. Which ones do you choose? At my current skill/knowledge level- Me: Psychic, Speed, Death For the Grim Reaper: Metal, Spirit, Light

Side question: using this card can you take an opponents card and put it in your row? Or is the key word “shift” mean only from your side?
Gnarly Charlie
Side question: using this card can you take an opponents card and put it in your row? Or is the key word “shift” mean only from your side?
Cambru 30/11/2025 20:18
It doesnt specify, which means either player
TheBigBoy 30/11/2025 20:18
You cannot steal their card
1
20:19
You can move their card to another stack on their side
20:19
“This line” refers to the whole lane, not just 1 side
20:20
I’m about to mix my main 1 and main 2 stuff so posting where I’m at to see how this changes when that integration happens
Gnarly Charlie 30/11/2025 20:20
Ah ok. So I can rearrange the line in either side but I cannot take a card from the opponent side and put it on my side. (edited)
20:20
Thank you
TheBigBoy 30/11/2025 20:21
Ya there’s currently no card that can steal a card that’s in play

rapanui 30/11/2025 20:27
Actually, that ties into a related question. Every protocol has the 5(Discard a Card). Has anyone noticed that the 5s work better in certain combinations? Like some combinations let you drop cards quickly, so it's easier to drop a 5 as your last play without it costing you extra. And if you're playing with a lot of flip-power, you can play your 5s facedown and flip them on your last play before refresh.
20:29
Also, I would argue Psychic has no 'sad tier' cards:
20:30
(I will also note that for 'beginner' games without Control, playing around Psychic 2 is extremely difficult.)

20:37

20:57

Michael Yang

TheBigBoy 01/12/2025 1:08

BlueMoons 01/12/2025 1:13
You can preemptively put points across from psychic (if they're playing with speed) or darkness (if playing with darkness, obvi) so playing facedowns gets you to compile faster
TheBigBoy 01/12/2025 2:04
But it can happen at any stage of the game, and if you’re playing suboptimally just to put points in those lines then you’re 1 rearrange away from a ton of lost value
2:05

Funny Samy 01/12/2025 2:12
Yeah the strongest stuff in the game is the kind of thing you worry about preemptively (cough mirror 1 cough)
rapanui 01/12/2025 2:46
In my first couple of games, I was trying to avoid refreshing for as long as possible, since any turn you don't develop your board is lost tempo. But I think refreshing from 1 or 2 is not horrible sometimes. What are people's thoughts on this?
TheBigBoy 01/12/2025 2:46
Yeah refreshing partially is usually better than making a mediocre play
2:47
But ofc depends a ton on the board and how urgent stuff is
Funny Samy 01/12/2025 3:04
I refresh from 1 and 2 a lot
3:05
If you get down to no cards in hand your opponent is guaranteed two turns in a row (barring some random card that gives you interaction on a refresh turn like fire 3 or death 1 or something)
3:07
A savvy opponent can exploit this by making an otherwise risky high-value play and compiling "for free" depending on board state
Cambru 01/12/2025 4:03
Sometimes playing all your cards out results in a couple of mediocre/low impact plays, which can also lose more tempo than an earlier refresh might have
rapanui 01/12/2025 4:18
OK, so... Control. I'm still not exactly sure how highly to value competing for it. Obviously, it's better to have it than to not, but tactically it is relatively low impact compared to just making generally strong, tempo-promoting plays. (aforementioned "play around psychic 2" issue notwithstanding) (edited)
bobjoejuan 01/12/2025 4:20
its amazing when both players have two protocols compiled
4:21
if you have control, your opponent has to stack onto their third protocol
4:21
while on the other hand you can compile in any lane, since with control, you can switch in the uncompiled protocol before compiling
4:26
what's the strategy for the top part of fear zero? "During your turn, your opponent´s cards do not have middle commands."
2
Davis 01/12/2025 4:30
I think it is mainly that you can delete, shift, and return without triggering powerful effects. Like, a savvy opponent might play a very high value card (maybe with a persistent effect or a 5) on top of a card with a very strong middle command, such that if you try to meddle with the strong card, you trigger their middle command
bobjoejuan 01/12/2025 4:32
hm i can see that, how very fearful indeed
rapanui
OK, so... Control. I'm still not exactly sure how highly to value competing for it. Obviously, it's better to have it than to not, but tactically it is relatively low impact compared to just making generally strong, tempo-promoting plays. (aforementioned "play around psychic 2" issue notwithstanding) (edited)
Khrono5 01/12/2025 4:51
having control is incredibly important, imagine your opponent has 10+ in a lane that is about to compile on their next turn, you can take an early refresh and force them to recompile a protocol instead of compliling a new protocol towards winning the game, being able to manipulate the protocols as needed is incredibly useful for that purpose as well as repositioning your own protocols as well. Good opponents wouldn't make a push in that situation but it basically acts a deterrent. (edited)
1
Funny Samy 01/12/2025 4:58
Yeah control means all lanes matter towards the end of the game and not just the ones that are yet uncompiled
TheBigBoy 01/12/2025 5:11
Control is the most important objective like 80% of the time after halfway through the game
5:11
You often can’t do anything without it
5:12
Or rather, if they have it
5:12
If they don’t have opp protocol rearrange then you can get away with it at neutral sometimes (edited)
rapanui
In my first couple of games, I was trying to avoid refreshing for as long as possible, since any turn you don't develop your board is lost tempo. But I think refreshing from 1 or 2 is not horrible sometimes. What are people's thoughts on this?
BlueMoons 01/12/2025 10:38
i think 3 feels almost always too early to refresh. I'd probably rather play a face-down in that case. If you last (and second-last) card in hand would lock you into a pretty fragile line of play, you're better off refreshing and giving initiative to your opponent so you can react with better cards.
rapanui
OK, so... Control. I'm still not exactly sure how highly to value competing for it. Obviously, it's better to have it than to not, but tactically it is relatively low impact compared to just making generally strong, tempo-promoting plays. (aforementioned "play around psychic 2" issue notwithstanding) (edited)
BlueMoons 01/12/2025 10:41
control is basically the biggest thing to play for when you're 2+ compiles into the game. for compiles 3-6 you need control to close out the game, if you don't have control your opponent is likely to just blow up your cards for the measly cost of 1 card from their deck.
rapanui 01/12/2025 14:16
I was misreading the control rules. You can rearrange any player's protocols. Makes a lot more sense now

Michael Yang 08/12/2025 1:50
I go with protocols that just dump a lot of value out. Things like Smoke and Life have enough internal synergy to keep them going even when they lose a few cards. Something like Fire also gives you more control over how you cycle your hand which could help you protect your cards a bit. Love and Assimilation are slow so you just rush them down. Also, when you lose a bunch of cards in 1 protocol, you'll just have to play face-down cards more aggressively to make up the difference.
2

AlienGX 18/12/2025 17:36
Can anyone explain how to properly play with <speed 0> ? I've played only a few games with Speed so far and was not able to create any useful situation with it... Am I dumb? Feels so bad to discard it or just play it for the sake of playing it...
AlienGX
Can anyone explain how to properly play with <speed 0> ? I've played only a few games with Speed so far and was not able to create any useful situation with it... Am I dumb? Feels so bad to discard it or just play it for the sake of playing it...
CompileBot BOT 18/12/2025 17:36
CompileBot v1.0.2
AlienGX
Can anyone explain how to properly play with <speed 0> ? I've played only a few games with Speed so far and was not able to create any useful situation with it... Am I dumb? Feels so bad to discard it or just play it for the sake of playing it...
makario 18/12/2025 17:39
You're not dumb. Speed 0 is a great card but not immediately obvious. I'll give you a few ways to use it. First, know that it pairs very well with <Speed 3>.
makario
You're not dumb. Speed 0 is a great card but not immediately obvious. I'll give you a few ways to use it. First, know that it pairs very well with <Speed 3>.
CompileBot BOT 18/12/2025 17:39
CompileBot v1.0.2
makario 18/12/2025 17:39
Like so:
  • Play Speed 0.
  • Play Speed 3 (and do it's middle action.)
  • For Speed 3's end step, shift itself. This will reveal Speed 0 and retrigger it, allowing you to play another card.
 (edited)
17:43
Let's say you've got Speed and Gravity now.
  • Speed 0, then play a card on top of it.
  • Use Gravity 1 to shift that card off of Speed 0, allowing you to play another card.
  • Alternatively, let's assume you played a powerful non-speed card face-down on top of Speed 0. Use Gravity 2 to first flip that card (triggering it), then shift it off, triggering Speed 0 again, allowing you to play another card.
 (edited)
AlienGX 18/12/2025 17:44
Ah thanks!! That makes sense. I always tried to make use of it playing the card. But you basically rather play it first and trigger it by manipulating/shifting, etc.
makario 18/12/2025 17:44
You can also use it defensively. Let's say you're at a total of 8 in the Speed line, but you know your opponent has a card that can delete one of your cards. You can play Speed 0, then play a card on top of it to bring your total to 10+. If they delete that card, it will allow you to play another card on top, so you can still compile.
bobjoejuan 18/12/2025 17:45
In Main 1 in particular, Speed 0 + some random card on top of it is very annoying to deal with

makario 01/01/2026 15:55
It depends. You're drafting, yes? Because in the situation above, if gravity + psychic is the issue you can at least choose one of them if it's giving you trouble. At a higher level, draft becomes important and while I'd lean toward picking a good combo for yourself, you also need to pick a little defensively if you expect trouble
1
Michael Yang 01/01/2026 19:47
When I'm learning to compete in a new game, I always practice how to "attack" first. This goes for a Compile, Go, Connect 4, Call of Duty, anything. In Compile, I'd say go for strong synergies first and learn how to push for compiles ASAP. In my experience, defensive learnings come more easily.
1
trojanrabbit 01/01/2026 22:02
Has anyone gathered enough data to look at first player advantage?
makario 01/01/2026 22:04
I think I have played enough games to say that it doesn't matter much.
22:09
(I'm assuming you mean within the game, after the draft.) There are some strong first play moves, sure. (Water 1, Psychic 2) But there are often situations where going second is advantageous. (Hate 0) I haven't run into any situation where it ultimately decided a game or considerably impacted tempo, though.
bobjoejuan 01/01/2026 22:10
well maybe picking a protocol first might matter
makario 01/01/2026 22:11
Drafting order matters a bit more, I think. Even that is dependent on the draft pool, though.
Alex Brogan 01/01/2026 22:35
But also, if you’re picking first you’re likely to pick a protocol that has strong first turn potential and second pick can counter pick.
22:36
I suspect having the power to pick the last protocol chosen has a lot of influence as well if you’re playing at a high level.
Alex Brogan
But also, if you’re picking first you’re likely to pick a protocol that has strong first turn potential and second pick can counter pick.
Eris 01/01/2026 23:41

I suspect having the power to pick the last protocol chosen has a lot of influence as well if you’re playing at a high level.

Eris 02/01/2026 23:31
I get the impression of it being one of the best cards in the game, probably because it has decided a lot of games in my experience. Another thing in compile is that I feel sometimes protocols are more than the sum of their parts. 23:32

Eris
I get the impression of it being one of the best cards in the game, probably because it has decided a lot of games in my experience. Another thing in compile is that I feel sometimes protocols are more than the sum of their parts. Since the deck is so small, cards get to retrigger or stay active for a long time and you get to just ignore some of your cards and discard them, sometimes a protocol will be as good as its 2 most useful cards in your game.

T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 04/01/2026 19:24
Im trying to find all the cards that have a "variable" value, or change the value of the lines to something other than the printed values on the cards. Is this list complete?:
  • Mirror 0
  • Diversity 3
  • Smoke 2
  • Clarity 0
  • Apathy 0
  • Darkness 2
  • Metal 0
Eris 04/01/2026 19:34
I think so?
makario 04/01/2026 20:01
I think so. I also think it's worth calling out that Darkness 2 is the only one that changes card value. Everything else changes the line value. So for example Diversity 3 is not "either a 3-value card or a 5-value card", it is always a 3-value card, even though your value in the line is increased by 2
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 04/01/2026 20:29
Thats a good point. I guess thats important for effects like Luck 4.

15:26

15:27

15:27

Eris 06/01/2026 16:29
I haven't played Main 2 a lot yet, so any opinions I have are more like first impressions. I think it's a weird one and I like it, although it fills a niche that doesn't have a lot of synergies right now. So your cards are always better if you have a small hand, specially 6 and 1. Peace 6 is pretty good because it doesn't have an adverse effect and it's not difficult to play in this protocol, so for now the function of Peace in my games has been to set up and then use the 6 to compile. Peace 1 can be really good in the right moment. All this was probably pretty obvious but whatever.
16:31
I think right now there aren't many protocols that have ton of synergy with discarding your own cards or having a small hand, but there are a few
16:35
Corruption comes to mind because of 2 and 6 (if you don't have cards you don't have to discard). It also gives you another 6, which I think can be pretty strong and has some tools to mess with your opponent's cards. I have yet to try it, but Time could also bee good, the idea here is that you have more ways to fill your own trash for Time 0,2 and 3. Also Courage because it gives you a 6 and Courage 0 can work great with Peace 1 and 6.
klenz
Note: some protocol by definition has to be the worst for each persons preferences and playstyles. And yeah i like METAL and APATHY much, much more compared to PEACE.
Eris 06/01/2026 16:36
Another Apathy fan
1
Austin (Puma) 06/01/2026 16:37
Peace is primarily a counterpick for me rather than a protocol you to build around. The tempo reset in 1 and tempo gains in 2 and 4 are good vs plague/war/psychic Peace isn't worried about small hand size so it can pair well with other decks that want to discard cards as it offers both discard targets and strong 6 value. I believe it's especially good with Corruption, and potentially Plague. It's one of the only protocols that has 1-6 power so if you can get your 6 into play you have one of the strongest power value protocols in the game.
1
Eris 06/01/2026 16:39
That actually makes a lot of sense, I haven't use it to counter discard protocols yet but Peace 4 only makes sense in that scenario
Austin (Puma) 06/01/2026 16:39
I believe Ice and Love are the only other two protocols that offer 1-6 power. Typically you lose your 4 for 6, although it does depend.
Eris 06/01/2026 16:40
Also Peace 2 can play in its own line so it can add 4 value (which is key if you have the 6)
Austin (Puma) 06/01/2026 16:41

Eris 06/01/2026 16:41

Austin (Puma) 06/01/2026 16:43
Generally because Peace just has one of the highest power value sets in the game but can be hard to get it fielded with the middle command limitation of 6. You could play it with Chaos or Time for the only other two covered self facedown to faceup flips. But because Peace 2 brings a face down play, that'd synergize better with Darkness than Chaos or Time imo

Alex Brogan 06/01/2026 18:48
Peace is odd to me because it seems to be designed around having as few cards in hand as possible, but that goes counter to the general strategy.
18:48
More cards = more info = more informed play = generally better outcomes
18:49
Peace is just less cards = fewer refreshes/flipping cards = better tempo?
18:49

18:50
<peace 3>
Alex Brogan
<peace 3>
CompileBot BOT 06/01/2026 18:50
CompileBot v1.0.2
Alex Brogan 06/01/2026 18:50
This one being a middle command rather than a top start command is the thing that I find most puzzling strategically.
18:52
You could feasibly combo a draw command with this while at a very low hand size to do some fun things, but it being a middle command makes that impossible without a really fragile flipping or shifting set up.
18:55
For example, with Chaos 0 and Peace 1 in play, you could draw and discard a card every turn to flip a card and still have a card to play without having to refresh if Peace 3 was a top start command.
18:56
Perhaps these kinds of things are why Peace feels underwhelming.
Eris 06/01/2026 23:34
I think it would be crazy strong if it was a rop command tbh
Funny Samy 07/01/2026 0:34
Yeah you are describing like fire 3 on turbo steroids
Alex Brogan 07/01/2026 3:10
I mean, it would still have its distinct limitation. You can’t just flip literally anything, and so much of peace is about discarding your stuff that you’d run out of cards pretty quickly outside of very specific setups that require at least 3 ongoing effects to maintain.
Austin (Puma) 07/01/2026 23:25
Maybe I'm just not playing against a high enough level of gameplay, but I've been playing a lot of peace recently and it's feeling really solid to me. -It's pure value numerically is quite strong -It recovers well vs discard decks through resetting tempo and bringing big values (does well defensively) -It combos very well alongside discard decks themselves (corruption, fire, plague, psychic etc; does well offesively) Is it S tier in all circumstances? Eh, probably not. But when mixed in with two other protocols, it more just gives extra value when I inevitably reach low card count rather than forcing me to burn to that and stay there.

Baxy125 12/01/2026 18:10
Hello! Does anyone have any beginner friendly protocol combination recommendations across all available? Specifically, 3 protocols for each player that teach mechanics, etc. Additionally, does anyone have any combinations of protocols that result in very nuanced gameplay? Appreciate any and all help!
Baxy125
Hello! Does anyone have any beginner friendly protocol combination recommendations across all available? Specifically, 3 protocols for each player that teach mechanics, etc. Additionally, does anyone have any combinations of protocols that result in very nuanced gameplay? Appreciate any and all help!
makario 12/01/2026 18:12
Do you have Main 1?
Funny Samy 12/01/2026 18:18
^ which sets do you have
makario 12/01/2026 18:20
I'd recommend Light, Spirit, Speed as first 3 because it teaches "flow" quite well.
  • What's the difference between top, middle, and bottom commands? Light has a bottom command. Spirit and Speed have top commands.
  • How do triggers work? Light has an "End" trigger, Spirit a "Start" trigger and a "Draw" trigger, Speed a "clear cache" trigger.
  • How do shifts and flips work? Plenty of cards in this protocol set to practice with Some questions that will likely come out of this set that are good thought exercises:
  • Is Speed 0 any good? I don't get why this matters
  • How do I best capitalize on Spirit 1, given that I have to discard at the start of every turn?
  • How many cards do I draw if I flip this card with Light 0?
 (edited)
5
makario 12/01/2026 18:27
Next, try Water, Darkness, Gravity. You will:
  • Learn about face-down play
  • Learn about returning cards
  • Learn about top-deck play (and discover times when you shouldn't play a card due to not having any in your deck) Some thought exercises:
  • Is Water 4 good? When would I ever want to return one of my own cards?
  • What order should I compile my protocols? (You should be playing with Control at this point)
  • Why is control important? (Water is a good protocol for control, while Gravity/Darkness greatly benefit from having it)
 (edited)
5
makario 12/01/2026 18:34
If you want to be a little more offensive/mean, try Plague instead of Spirit/Speed in that first game. It has top/bottom commands and makes your opponent discard. (edited)
1
Funny Samy 12/01/2026 18:36
This makes a ton of sense
dgbm 12/01/2026 19:02
In addition to makario's excellent recommendations, Life-Water-Darkness vs. Spirit-Gravity-Speed is a fun matchup I think, and while not terribly complex, both sets have interesting synergies, and counterplay options.
19:09
And this is likely obvious, but I'd strongly recommend starting with Main 1, and reading the Compile Codex.

TheBigBoy 13/01/2026 2:38

TMind
Hi guys, I was able to play the Psychic "only play cards facedown" into my darkness covered cards. My opponent was furious . Since it is a known combo, how to deal with it , and can it be game breaking ?
Austin (Puma) 13/01/2026 4:15
It's obviously strong but with the setups being very specific I believe it can be fought against. -playing strong against the lines where the combo is likely to take place reduces the number of turns playing face down due to compiling -playing high disruption protocols to reduce the chance of the combo being available. Prevent facedown cards from staying in lines that are likely to have the combo by moving or deleting them. Playing cards face down is generally losing a player tempo so disrupting face down cards will likely gain you tempo (or at least be close to even). Fire, death and hate are great for deleting. Gravity light and smoke for shifting -prevent abilities from going off or cards from being played in a line with apathy, plague or to a lesser extent ice.
1
4:16
-swap lots of cards between decks to break up the combo with love, chaos or assimilation
1
4:18
-mess with your opponents protocol positions with spirit, chaos or using control
1
4:20
-corruption 0 played on a line you suspect to become an option for the combo could very well shut it right down as well
1
Funny Samy 13/01/2026 4:30
Unfortunately I think main 2 makes the setup a lot less specific
4:31
Like time and chaos can flip covered in any lane
dgbm 13/01/2026 7:16
The odds of drawing a specific combination of two cards in your opening hand is 6.5% (says the mighty Deck-u-lator): https://deckulator.appspot.com/
7:21
Therefore, if you're expecting the combo to be played against you, be mindful that you're on a clock - draft protocols that go fast and hard, and work towards being able to compile the Darkness line easily with face-down cards. (edited)
7:22
Take mental notes when the opponent is refreshing/drawing/looking like they're trying to set the thing up.
7:25
Going first helps in general; as others have pointed out, Hate 4 is a good answer, as well as Speed 3 on Hate 0 or 1 (basically shift Speed 3 at End to uncover whatever nastiness is waiting there).
7:27
Yes, they might be able to play around whatever you're doing, but that likely means that they're not setting up the combo, so play for initiative and know your outs.
7:28
If you have Main 2 - draft Time+Corruption+Fear and laugh at them (with kindness ).
Austin (Puma)
-corruption 0 played on a line you suspect to become an option for the combo could very well shut it right down as well
Eris 13/01/2026 16:11
When I saw that card I thought it was a specific counter to this combo
16:14
I would say that for Main 1 combos (Psychic+Speed or Darkness) Plague can be good to prepare, Plague 4 can disrupt the combo so maybe save that card if you think the opponent might play it. Plague 0 is also good and making your opponent discard can disrupt it before they can play it, since they'll be holding the cards
16:16
I've also seen Fire being suggested, since if your opponent gets to play the combo Fire 3 can let you flip yor cards face up. Something similar with Fire 0
1
16:19
Lots of good suggestions, although it's still really luck dependent, not only if your opponent gets to play it, but also where and when. It can perfectly hapoen that you just lost the game pretty much.
Eris
Lots of good suggestions, although it's still really luck dependent, not only if your opponent gets to play it, but also where and when. It can perfectly hapoen that you just lost the game pretty much.
dgbm 13/01/2026 16:29
It's definitely a strong combo, yes! But Compile in general is a pretty high-power game I think, and covered Psychic 1 definitely is more on the feel-bad side, which I absolutely respect, but I also believe it's not egregious.
16:34
And "luck dependent" is sort of in the dna of card games to an extent, you'll ocassionally lose to any combination of protocols because of bad draws; I think with Psychic 1, the elephant in the room is whether it's too good - I believe it's OK, and I get that some people might want to house rule it, but I also think that that's not a good reason for official changes (not that anyone's proposing that, just thinking out loud).
Eris 13/01/2026 16:51

5:59

5:59

6:00
like 2nd player starts with Control, etc.
6:01

Anthony Rubbo (lemonyfresh42) 14/01/2026 6:08
Last note from @Michael Yang was: "In short though, I think the important part to consider, is that some protocols are better proactively and other reactively. And, with completely random decks, variance is high enough that 2nd player starting with control feels irrelevant or just as likely to overpower the 2nd player over the 1st in a game with less optimized decks." Just trying to clarify -- for the first part, is the claim that some decks would prefer to defer going first to the other deck, if they had they option?
6:13
And for the 2nd part re: high-variance -- If there is a consensus that FPA exists, I do not see why variance should be used as a measure of whether or not to counterbalance it.
Funny Samy 14/01/2026 6:18

6:18

6:18

Anthony Rubbo (lemonyfresh42)
And for the 2nd part re: high-variance -- If there is a consensus that FPA exists, I do not see why variance should be used as a measure of whether or not to counterbalance it.
makario 14/01/2026 6:54
No consensus that FPA exists. In fact, I lean towards it not being a thing. There are benefits to going second. (Flips, deletes need a target)
Anthony Rubbo (lemonyfresh42) 14/01/2026 7:52
Are those truly benefits, over going first?
7:56
For how many matchups would you allow the other player to start with Control, so that you may go first? For how many matchups would you allow the other player to start with Control, ao that you may go second?
makario 14/01/2026 8:20
It can be, but really not enough to matter either way. Your questions give weight to my stance. I would never choose to allow the other player to start with control so I could go first or second (edited)
8:27
While I do find it pretty interesting that you hit a 7-streak of 1st-player wins, I think you'll see it matter less and less as you put in more. But I'm curious: why do you think that it is an advantage? What cards or situations or positions have you found that benefit from going first (or even second)? (edited)
Anthony Rubbo (lemonyfresh42)
Last note from @Michael Yang was: "In short though, I think the important part to consider, is that some protocols are better proactively and other reactively. Just trying to clarify -- for the first part, is the claim that some decks would prefer to defer going first to the other deck, if they had they option?
Eris 14/01/2026 8:58
Death is a good example, there's not a Death card that would like to go first aside from Death 1 and even that one would prefer being played on top of another card with a powerful middle effect if possible. Similar with Hate. (edited)
9:04
If your opponent goes first and you have the ability to disrupt a good value card or ruin a good setup you might be in a better spot overall than if you had started having to waste a resource that would've been more useful later.
9:05

yeah generally death is more powerful than hate, but hate is more versatile

Anthony Rubbo (lemonyfresh42) 14/01/2026 11:24
From Michael: "Favorite mod for experienced players: 2nd Player Starts In Control As players get very skilled at Compile, more and more of the battle takes place during the draft. When doing some sort of limited draft, this can become doubly troublesome for the 2nd player. If you’re in this situation, try giving the 2nd player control at the start of the game. It’s a small change but it makes player 2’s first refresh more powerful, allowing them to rearrange their protocols to lean into their counter-picks, giving them an advantage in the next round of back-and-forth."
Funmaker 14/01/2026 11:28
I would think FPA probably exists, but I think it isn’t very big of a deal. I think when comparing to magic the gathering, you can see: the slower the format, the less it matters. Just simple math: A game lasts 5 turns: P1 played 50% more turns than P2 A game lasts 51 turns: P1 played 2% more turns than P2
11:29
I honestly think picking protocols first is the more relevant advantage
Anthony Rubbo (lemonyfresh42) 14/01/2026 11:34
Re: simple math -- not necessarily. It could be the exact opposite, depending on the system. Through compuond interest, a small early advantage can balloon to a ridiculous advantage 50 turns later, whereas as 5 turns later it may not have materialized to much.
Funmaker 14/01/2026 11:38
Yeah I think that’s another problem with magic. Snowballing out of control. But since you always play exactly one card each turn in compile. This isn’t the case imo
11:39

henri 14/01/2026 13:26
Playing ~4% less cards (based on my average game length) does neither seem insignificant nor huge. Much more impactful is pickorder in draft. Assuming rules as written with no bans in the 15 (14 if we're being honest and not counting metal) cards of MN01+AX01, first rights to one protocol does not feel nearly as valueable as one more direct counterpick, picking two of the strongest protocols right away and having the last pick open to complete the best deck into the opponent's protocols, after seing their full composition.
Funmaker 14/01/2026 14:33

henri 14/01/2026 15:18
I'm curious to see a full digital implementation of the game. Even though the cards look amazing and i love the feeling of having actual cards at a table, an automatic system, would simplify so much:
  • easy native implementation of a chess clock, automatically passing time every time a choice needs to be made
  • solves shuffling on a timer
  • prompts you on every action in the correct order so accidental rule transgressions made, or forgetting about effects would just naturally decrease to zero
(- play anywhere on the phone even with no opponent closeby)

henri 14/01/2026 15:19

Anthony Rubbo (lemonyfresh42)
From Michael: "Favorite mod for experienced players: 2nd Player Starts In Control As players get very skilled at Compile, more and more of the battle takes place during the draft. When doing some sort of limited draft, this can become doubly troublesome for the 2nd player. If you’re in this situation, try giving the 2nd player control at the start of the game. It’s a small change but it makes player 2’s first refresh more powerful, allowing them to rearrange their protocols to lean into their counter-picks, giving them an advantage in the next round of back-and-forth."
makario 14/01/2026 15:49
Ah, right. I've been talking about this just in the context of turn order, ignoring the draft. Draft order is a different story, in fact the same conversation happened several days ago Draft order does matter in the context of limited draft, and quite a bit. We've had to do a bit of lab work in our games to figure out how to keep things interesting by forcing constraints through limiting options, without limiting them so much that it becomes a solved problem during the draft. But in those games we played where the draft was too limited, it wasn't always going first that gave you the advantage. Sometimes you'd end up having to choose the lesser of two evils while your opponent gets a guaranteed troublesome combo. We even swapped to the coin flip winner choosing to go first or second because of this, but ultimately the solution was to increase the limit to allow for more flexibility and creativity.
Funny Samy 14/01/2026 15:50
I think we have to wait for tournaments to happen and use the data from there to get a sense of how much these things matter
Funny Samy
I think we have to wait for tournaments to happen and use the data from there to get a sense of how much these things matter
dgbm 14/01/2026 16:33
I'm afraid obtaining reasonably large data sets is going to be impossible without the digital version.
Michael Yang 14/01/2026 17:59
Stats on first player advantage, protocol pick and win rates, and retrigger rates will be very interesting to see. But, honestly, there's only one card I think about changing for balance reasons. And it just so happens that the balance problem is a fun problem too which draws my attention to it. If there's a table that picks Death every game because of its power, I think that's awesome. At my table Death doesn't get picked as often and that's a cool dichotomy. It tells me that there's a lot of space for individual expression. And, not to denigrate game balance (I also appreciate highly competitive dueling card games), but facilitating player expression is more important to me as a designer than "perfect" balance. Eyes and ears are always open though. Play more games and let me know!
3
1

TMind 26/01/2026 17:36
I've had some first games with Aux 1, and I was wondering how to use Apathy. I only came up with darkness as a good partner and maybe flip heavy protocols like gravity. It also might serve as a counter to hidden top card abilities. What are your takes on it?
Eris 26/01/2026 17:47
I really like Apathy, but my impression is that it's probably the weakest protocol in Wave 1. I've tried to make it work and I've had some success with it, but it has a couple things that make it play against its own interest. Mainly, the fact that Apathy 1, which is probably its strongest card, also flips your own cards, this means that you can't play your 4,5,0 or 2 before it if you want them to be effective. But for Apathy 1 to be effective, you also need your opponent to have useful material in that line that you can flip face-down.
17:48
Setting aside Apathy 1 and looking at the other cards:
17:53
My take is that Apathy 0 is actually the worst one and it's kind of a trap. It makes you think you want to play around putting a lot of face-down cards, but it's really difficult that you'll get more than 3-4 value from it. It has to be played specifically after Apathy 1 in a line where you and your opponent have a bunch of cards for that to happen. I'm not saying it's impossible to use it, just really difficult to get good value.
17:55
Apathy 2 and 3 are pretty good at controlling your opponent. A cool interaction with Apathy 2 I learned recently is that you can do something similar to the Psychic 1 combo and flip it with Darkness 2.
17:57
Apathy 4 isn't particularly great but if you use it to flip your 1 or a 0 it gives you 5-6 points without making you discard so it's nice.
18:02
Anyway, I'm curious to know about other people's experiences here, so far the most success I've had with it is to use it as a disrupting protocol against things like Hate, Speed or Death, and try to make use of Apathy 1 and 2 as much as I can. The 0 can be nice if you end up drawing it in a good moment and maybe it can get you to 10 after you have flipped everything, but it's not the key component. Maybe I'm wrong and I can learn to play it better, but it's been hard to win games with it most of the time.

I think apathy is meant to be control oriented but I don't have much success with it
1

5:10

Motley D. Krewffy 27/01/2026 9:35
@Michael Yang, I'm really curious about the Metal Protocol. I heard you like combining it with Life and Water, but I really don't understand it. What's good about Metal? To me, it seems really weak, but I so badly wanna be proven wrong and see something cool.
Motley D. Krewffy
@Michael Yang, I'm really curious about the Metal Protocol. I heard you like combining it with Life and Water, but I really don't understand it. What's good about Metal? To me, it seems really weak, but I so badly wanna be proven wrong and see something cool.
fruit 27/01/2026 12:43
Hey, you didn't ping me but I hope it's ok if I still give my opinion about Metal. It's a very versatile control protocol that pairs well with a couple of others due to metal 0 also allowing you to flip. Water and Life bank a lot on flipping cards. This can both be used to negate opponents control attempts, like a psychic protocol or be used to control the opponent and negate their 0s and 1s. In addition to that metal 0 has an ability to reduce the opponents total value of a line by 2. Another way to think about this is that this card essentially can grant you 2 points in value while also letting you flip a card (like spirit 2) so you have extra power in there. So the combination with either water, life or spirit has really good synergy! Metal 2 is underrated in my opinion. It controls protocols that want to either play face down cards like darkness and it prevents some combos that work with placing a card face down and flipping them in a different protocol. All that while being a top box effect. Also you cannot play face down cards if you have fewer hand cards to force a compilation. It's really solid. Metal 1 is honestly bonkers when it comes to granting time. The 2 extra cards while denying a compilation gives you a chance to top the enemies value in a line.
3
fruit 27/01/2026 12:50
Oh, metal 0 is better than spirit 2 because it can bump down the opponent out of compilation range and is better for gaining control next turn
1

13:49

13:51

13:52

fruit 27/01/2026 13:58
it for sure can be picked as a counter. My thought process is that since it has many good synergies you can pick it without revealing your strategy to player 2

Impalsi 27/01/2026 14:01
comboing it with something that incidentally adds cards to that line (like Water 1 or Life 0) seems helpful. ideally, you want it to delete 8+ of your opp's cards and none of yours, but that's not realistic. but also, you don't want to be committing a bunch of cards to a line you're going to delete. so i could see things like W1/L0 lowering that cost. I could also imagine possibly comboing it with Spirit 1 to let you play 0s and 1s in the line you wish to delete as another way of upping the card count, getting good middle command activations as you go, while still not being too sad about deleting them when the time comes (edited)

fruit 08/02/2026 22:48

Guzel
I am getting smoked every game, what are some things I should look out for? (not really a beginner, got like 20-30 games played, but my opponent just always cooks me and I don't get it)
Michael Yang 08/02/2026 22:50
If you play consistently with 1 opponent, I'd ask them what you could do better. I find that the best way to learn. Something like "When we were in this part of the game, I did this move. Was that the right thing to do? Did that make you happy or scared?" or "You did this move. Why did you do that?
Guzel 08/02/2026 22:53
That's such a good call. Usually I am not as locked in and forget everything that happened five seconds after, but I'll make sure I do it the next time

Impalsi 08/02/2026 22:59
Tough to know what would be most helpful for you specifically without playing with you (which we could play sometime if you'd like), but some general beginner/intermediate-level mindset things that come to my mind:
  • Control is extremely important. Often, in the mid and late game, it's the thing that matters most. So pay close attention to it. Play to get it, play to keep it, play to not let your opponent get it.
  • Because of the importance of control, it follows that it's often better to think in terms of getting max points onto the board (or removing max points from your opponent) rather than thinking in terms of rushing to compilation.
  • Try to utilize your cards to their fullest potential, but not too much! Some cards can be so profoundly powerful (Death 2, Death 0, Water 3, Light 3, etc.) but require a particular setup. It's a tricky balance to strike -- waiting for just the right moment vs. playing it under just-okay circumstances -- but be aware of the potential of these cards and try to chart a course toward maximizing them. This is a really tough one to give general advice on, but something to start paying attention to if nothing else: Did I get the most out of my cards?
  • If none of your plays seem very good, refreshing is always an option! I often encounter a zugzwang feeling where I don't really want to make any move on the current board. Refreshing can be good in such a situation. As I've gotten more confident in the game and started playing against stronger opponents, I've noticed myself and my strong opponents refreshing earlier and more often.
 (edited)
1

Eris 10/02/2026 18:35
I feel like Darkness is a protocol that can go with almost anything because it does a bit of everything and also is pretty self-reliable.
  • It gives some decent opponent control if you need that (this can fit into any comp)
  • goes really well with face down protocols. Darkness 2 obviously, but Darkness 4 can give you a free retrigger. It also goes well with Light, that doesn't play a lot of face down cards but uses them (Dark 3+Light 0 or 2, Light 0+ Dark 4, Dark 2+ Light 3)
  • if you don't play a face down deck, Darkness 4 can still give good interaction to retrigger a good 0 or 1
  • good with Psychic but only really because of the Psy 1 combo
  • Darkness 2 is one of the best at rushing compiles, I think this also makes it good with Gravity, which is similar.
fruit
I tried that too. I dont yet understand how to make it work
Eris 10/02/2026 18:37
The problem with Light-Apathy I think is that apathy usually covers its face down cards, so it doesn't give good interaction to Light 0 and 2 (edited)
Eris
I feel like Darkness is a protocol that can go with almost anything because it does a bit of everything and also is pretty self-reliable.
  • It gives some decent opponent control if you need that (this can fit into any comp)
  • goes really well with face down protocols. Darkness 2 obviously, but Darkness 4 can give you a free retrigger. It also goes well with Light, that doesn't play a lot of face down cards but uses them (Dark 3+Light 0 or 2, Light 0+ Dark 4, Dark 2+ Light 3)
  • if you don't play a face down deck, Darkness 4 can still give good interaction to retrigger a good 0 or 1
  • good with Psychic but only really because of the Psy 1 combo
  • Darkness 2 is one of the best at rushing compiles, I think this also makes it good with Gravity, which is similar.

Michael Yang 15/02/2026 3:08
For the tournament I'm running, I'm going to use 2 full sets of all Compile products for 12 players. This means that each of the 6 tables will get 10 protocols. Instead of distributing protocols randomly, I thought it'd be fun to create the protocol lineups per table. The process ended up being interesting so I thought I'd share and take feedback before locking it in. A1 Darkness, Gravity, Metal, Light, Hate, Time, Chaos, Courage, Fear, Unity A2 Psychic, Water, Plague, Spirit, Love, Diversity, Clarity, Mirror, Peace, War A3 Speed, Life, Death, Fire, Apathy, Smoke, Assimilation, Corruption, Luck, Ice B1 Darkness, Water, Life, Apathy, Metal, Assimilation, Smoke, Time, Chaos, War B2 Psychic, Gravity, Plague, Hate, Light, Unity, Peace, Mirror, Corruption, Fear B3 Speed, Fire, Death, Spirit, Love, Diversity, Luck, Clarity, Courage, Ice

fruit 18/02/2026 20:38
to all of the apathy enthusiasts on this server (everyone, clearly) apathy, darkness, light CAN win you games. Is it top tier and is going to carry you through competetive draft formats? No, it will not, but in some matchups it might be strong. I am down for more testing in some casual games :>
20:43
Apathy and Darkness is kind of obvious and I am not the first one to think of this. There is clearly synergy there. So what about Light? The idea is that you would want to play apathy cards face down into darkness and vice verca IF you were able to consistently flip say a darkness 2 into apathy or apathy 0 into darkness. Light 0, 2 and 3 are actually made for that! and the protocoll provides draw which is great since you do not want to rely solely on darkness 0 for that. Additionally light 4 is really good for Apathy 2 to actually get some use out of it. It's really fun ^^ (edited)
1
20:45
oh and the covered card flips of darkness and apathy get way more use with light 2 and 3 in play

TMind 18/02/2026 22:08

Eris
Why do you think it's a Love counter?
fruit 18/02/2026 22:10
First of all, stealing apathy with love just feels bad. Unless you play darkness or gravity yourself the cards are going to be worthless at best. If you steal apathy 0 there is still darkness 2 to compensate and vice versa. Yes it hurts, love is always gonna do love things, but it doesn't end your game right then and there. Love helps you draw cards. Even though that is nothing special and comes with a cost, it is one of darkness and apathies biggest downsides and therefore you can make great use out of it. Apathy 2 is the most effective against love because love almost runs exclusively with middle effects. So if you play apathy 2 face down and flip it while covered it can render love useless for a time (light 4 helps with timing). Last but not least apathy 2 can make love 6 the best 6 card when someone's giving it to you with love 3 which can be a genuine strategy. This is the weakest advantage and nothing to bank on but it is there. (edited)

it seems you're from DE too

Slight 19/02/2026 6:10
Okay, here is an initial tierlist based on my plays of Compile thus far (50ish games). Curious what yall think, here, as I know I am off on some of these b/c I cant play enough! S - Hate, Psychic (always draft, goes well with most protocols and defines any deck they are apart of) A - Chaos, Gravity, Darkness, Time, Fire, Clarity, Courage (Powerful Build-around, Powerful self-synergy, or High-Flexibility) B - Peace, Assimilation, Corruption, Water, Death, Smoke, Spirit, Ice, Unity, Speed, Life, Fear, Mirror (Statsticks, Powerful but inflexible, or Counters powerful Protocols) C - Light, War, Diversity, Luck, Plague, Love (Narrow but effective Counterplay, Inconsistent Power Level) D - Apathy, Metal (Weak) So what am I getting wrong here? Note: I always play draft from 9 (never full draft). This might be important context for my ratings, since Protocols like Speed and Time are MUCH better when you can draft the specific combo pieces you want. (edited)
1
makario 19/02/2026 7:43
(1) You're missing a protocol (2) Not really sure one can be "wrong" with personal tier lists (3) I find your choices interesting and would love some elaboration, but I understand if you're not up for essay writing. Might be easier if we play and chat sometime

fruit 19/02/2026 11:34
yeah but like you said, psy 1 is hard to play around. As far as I know, only speed,darkness and chaos can set it up. 11:34
I think returning cards is actually the most usefull when you return your own 0s
Eris 19/02/2026 11:37

most of the time you can just play the returned card again and the opponent runs out of steam after that especially if you trigger a revealed middle box underneath the returned card.
11:38

I think it is good if you return a card and reveal a 5 or return a 5 or 6 on top of a face down card

fruit 19/02/2026 12:54
My main argument besides the inconsistency of it's individual cards besides psy 0 is that psy 1 does not work without support and the support options that exist do not have much synergy at all. you either play for psy 1 where darkness is best but doesn't help in other ways psychic would like or you don't and play it for discard like plague and then accept that psy 1 is pretty useless. I love playing psychic casually and pulling it off is one of the most rewarding feelings in the whole game but it is just not consistent enough :/ (edited)

12:56

fruit
why do you think it is stronger than psy 0?
Eris 19/02/2026 13:24
It's an unconditional shift of one of your opponent's cards, which means you can shift their 4-5 to force them to recompile. It also has a value of 3 so it's more likely that you'll be able to deny them control.
13:28
Psy 0 is nice but since it doesn't give any points, in the end you are using a turn to draw 2 cards and make them discard 2. You also make them reveal their hand which is pretty good, but a lot of times you get to see 0-2 cards and they get to play one of them/refresh before you can really use that information (edited)
makario 19/02/2026 13:29
My alternate wording for Psychic 0 is "Draw 2 cards. Your opponent refreshes"
1
Eris 19/02/2026 13:30
It does have retrigger value like it's a good card but I feel like it tends to feel like you are doing more than you actually are, while Psy 3 is underrated (edited)
13:31

makario
My alternate wording for Psychic 0 is "Draw 2 cards. Your opponent refreshes"

Only the refresh doesnt happen right away but consumes the opponents turn
makario 19/02/2026 13:31

13:32

Slight 19/02/2026 15:09

fruit
Why do you think psychic goes well with most protocols?
Slight 19/02/2026 15:17
Psychic 0 - Tempo Play to force Refresh (3/5) 1 - Insane highrolls with certain combos, otherwise relarively weak. Highroll potential makes taking this protocol 1st in a draft forces opponent to draft your combo pieces, which damages their better plan of taking powerful protocols. Remember I always play draft from 9. (4/5 for its impact on draft and high roll) 2 - Super strong. Easy to force a re-compile against stats-heavy protocols. Huge tempo, can sometimes force a recompile -> refresh for two weak turns in a row with their hands tied. (5/5) 3 - Very strong for a 3 value. Can often be a game-winning move (5/5) 4 - Very strong value for a 4. Being able to leave this out makes it super flexible. Gives you an "extra action" on a turn for high tempo. Ideally, youre using this on the turn you attwmpt to Compile. (4/5) (edited)
15:18
(*/5) is my individual card rating) These card ratings are based on other carda at the same power. (edited)

Psychic 0 - Tempo Play to force Refresh (3/5) 1 - Insane highrolls with certain combos, otherwise relarively weak. Light 2 also gives an advantage of 2 cards but also gives me a very flexible and potentially useful effect. Plague 2 gives me less tempo, but lets me adapt to how many cards I need my opponent to discard and trying to compile with it is much more consistent.

Slight 19/02/2026 16:43
I started re-reading the series over after book 5 (edited)
16:43
Okay, Clarity! IMHO, this is a powerful build-around Protocol, whos power-level will be dependent upon the available draft pool. The halmark of a good Clarity deck is (a) Card Draw, and (b) above-rate 1-value cards. Card Draw is important because it lets you deck manipulate with Clarity 1. Card Draw and built-in Refreshes are important because they are 1-to-1 with power for Clarity 0. Ideally, our game-plan with Clarity is to spin plates (part of why Life is a good pairing) and surprise our opponent with a multi-pronged attack on refresh/card-draw with Clarity 0 gaining ~4 power.
16:43
Whoops, hit enter early, I have more to say! Got distracted with Coltaine!

makario 19/02/2026 16:47
The (2) argument is really just me trying to ask you to play with me. I could less subtle. I said what I said about Psychic because of your explanation, I still think it's rated too highly. But explanations like that help me understand what people value or don't value in their playstyles--you are likely valuing discarding higher than I would, and not valuing control enough. (I can't remember the last time Psychic 2's rearrange hurt me.) Just an FYI, if you're wanting to use the bot to learn cards, there's a #🤖│bot-commands channel for that (edited)
1
1
Slight
Okay, Clarity! IMHO, this is a powerful build-around Protocol, whos power-level will be dependent upon the available draft pool. The halmark of a good Clarity deck is (a) Card Draw, and (b) above-rate 1-value cards. Card Draw is important because it lets you deck manipulate with Clarity 1. Card Draw and built-in Refreshes are important because they are 1-to-1 with power for Clarity 0. Ideally, our game-plan with Clarity is to spin plates (part of why Life is a good pairing) and surprise our opponent with a multi-pronged attack on refresh/card-draw with Clarity 0 gaining ~4 power.
Slight 19/02/2026 17:06
There are quite a few protocols which really benefit from Clarity 2’s plan, so Clarity 2 is usually just a 3 power card with a huge amount of upside, as well as the ability to manipulate the deck. 1-Power cards are usually very powerful. Most of the power-level of Clarity is going to be centered around your ability to “go wide”, gain the control element, and threaten your opponent in unexpected ways (with Clarity 0). Clarify 4 is pretty weak, but has self-synergy to set up Clarity 2/3. Clarity 3 is stronger than it looks imho, since it is good card advantage and basically gives you an even higher chance of drawing answers (instead of 5s) That’s my take on Clarity, it’s been really good with Spirit, Life, Hate, Water And I think it will be good with Assimilation, Courage, Mirror, Fear (but haven’t tried yet) (edited)
makario
The (2) argument is really just me trying to ask you to play with me. I could less subtle. I said what I said about Psychic because of your explanation, I still think it's rated too highly. But explanations like that help me understand what people value or don't value in their playstyles--you are likely valuing discarding higher than I would, and not valuing control enough. (I can't remember the last time Psychic 2's rearrange hurt me.) Just an FYI, if you're wanting to use the bot to learn cards, there's a #🤖│bot-commands channel for that (edited)

BlueMoons 20/02/2026 9:48
Face-down cards are generally just worse than face-up cards. They're worth 1.5 points less on average, and they don't have text. You still want to play them sometimes but you do not want to be playing face-down from hand without a plan.
TMind 20/02/2026 9:48

RepoRogue 20/02/2026 15:57

Slight 22/02/2026 16:43
#bestcombo Okay everyone, what do yall think in the best all-around 3 protocol combo (using all available protocols)? If youre not sure (like me), then just put your best guess! No wrong answers, just fun! My submission: Plague, Corruption, Water Why do I think this? Plague 3, Water 3, and Corruption 0 give well-rounded counterplay for niche opponents. Plague and Corruption have powerful discard synergies, which combo well with retriggers from Water 4. Corrution 1, Water 0, and Plague 0 give good control options on top of our niche counters above. Limitations: Might struggle to get power on the board sometimes, but Corruption 6 is a really good 6 when Corruption 2 is out. Might need to have a good sense of when to force discards, which requires higher skill. (edited)
