Eris 23/02/2026 8:51
I think this is one of the reasons why looking at a protocol (or even at a card) as the sum of straight values is not the way to rank them.
8:53
The thing is, to which extent Love having some bad cards makes the protocol bad when you are expected to give cards to your opponent? Love players almost always give you the 6 from the begginning. If we say Love 6 is bad then it will be a bad card your opponent will have. (edited)
Funny Samy
I feel like love is one of the hardest protocols to assess the strength of
Eris 23/02/2026 8:56
I agree, and as a non Love player I've underestimated it a lot. I think it's difficult to rank because the whole "which cards to give and when" requires a lot of experience. Some people seem to prefer getting rid of their 6 and and some 5s, but I think Love 6 tends to come back to you like a boomerang in the late game. Would ig be better to steal as much high value cards from your opponent as you can? Idk (edited)
8:58
Anyway, I think Love 1 gives the protocol a lot of potential, the games just get really difficult in the late game when you have 12 cards on your deck
Eris
I agree, and as a non Love player I've underestimated it a lot. I think it's difficult to rank because the whole "which cards to give and when" requires a lot of experience. Some people seem to prefer getting rid of their 6 and and some 5s, but I think Love 6 tends to come back to you like a boomerang in the late game. Would ig be better to steal as much high value cards from your opponent as you can? Idk (edited)
BlueMoons 23/02/2026 11:59
just handing out your high value cards is not great because it makes it harder to get control if your opponent just has cards with more points
Eris 23/02/2026 13:31
You are probably right. Another important thing about Love is that it's the only protocol that has a 6 instead of a 0. I see Love 1 (and the protocol as a hole probably) as similar to Spirit 1 or Plague 0 in the sense that it's pretty hard to measure their value on paper. (edited)
Eris
The thing is, to which extent Love having some bad cards makes the protocol bad when you are expected to give cards to your opponent? Love players almost always give you the 6 from the begginning. If we say Love 6 is bad then it will be a bad card your opponent will have. (edited)

And even if their average card is 20% worse than yours, they’re way ahead on board so they can still close the game
Impalsi 23/02/2026 14:55
It's not at all clear to me how powerful Love is, but I think at its best it can totally neuter the opposing deck. It does seem extremely skill testing, as understanding what cards to give your opponent is very complicated and has a ton of variables to consider (current board state pressures, long term stuff). But if the game goes on long enough, the hope is that you can turn your opponent's deck into trash and take all their best cards. The way it's been explained to me, you want to pair it with protocols that slow the game down to give you time to reach this state
14:56
I didn't understand this big picture strategy at all until I had it done to me recently in a tournament match. That made it click - what's possible and what the game plan is
1
TheBigBoy
Yeah by the time you achieve good-card-density-supremacy the game is basically over
RepoRogue 23/02/2026 15:21
I agree with this analysis and also, hi! That's a name I haven't seen in a while.
1
RepoRogue 23/02/2026 15:29
I'm very much not sold on Love in general. The idea of giving your opponent bad cards requires you to draft bad cards and hope you can give them to your opponent. Getting a random card from your opponent isn't particularly high value. The best case I can see for it is disrupting Unity, but then you can't really play the Unity cards without enabling their free compile. I'd lean towards the strength of Love (if there is one) being raw value plays. Having a 6 in your deck instead of a 0 means that you can compile with two cards. Love has 21 points of value to the typical Protocol's 15. I'm not sold that's enough, especially when the 6 is giving your opponent cards. But I'd still rather play it than give it to my opponent. Assimilation seems more effective as a combo disruptor and at least has the potential to do really wild things with Assimilation 2 and shift effects. The 6 being able to disrupt certain effects on your opponent's side while being a decent tempo play is also quite nice. I think TBB is 100% correct. Any game where you can really shift the quality of your deck in your favor is going to be over before that matters. And the whole theory rests on the idea that your opponent started with a better deck than you did, which is undesirable! I could see Love being a good choice in a 9 Protocol draft with 3 really strong Protocols where your opponent gets 2 of them.
1
1
henri 23/02/2026 15:33
Love has much greater average point value due to being 123456. Love 6 isn't bad at all, not many other cards allow you to just slam 6 net value points. Drawn cards are potential for later, but that doesn't matter if that moment you win. Love 3 is such an amazing threat for surgical disassembly, protection, and paranoia. The cards aren't even bad, they all have at least average, often better effects than others of their value. The one outlier is love 1, which is just absolutely terrible by value. Sure you have a minor chance to steal that one card your opponent really needed... but you can't manipulate that chance. So while it does have that one pull at a slot machine, it is in essence a waste which I try to get rid of. (edited)
15:37
Love in my eyes is pure tempo and a very solid protocol choice.
TheBigBoy 23/02/2026 15:58
The best pairing with love is fire imo
15:59
Because the discard costs of fire synergize with 1 and 2, and let you discard the bad cards for value (3 and sometimes 6)
16:00
So the dream is love, fire and then a 3rd one that has high variation in card power (speed maybe?)
16:00
The idea being you have a few super high power cards balanced by some duds, and then you discard or donate the duds
RepoRogue 23/02/2026 16:03
I think I'd pick Ice over Love most of the time. Love 4 is phenomenal, but I think Ice has more consistent card quality with the same value spread. I overall prefer Ice 6 to Love 6 and find the downside pretty easy to play around.
Eris 23/02/2026 16:09
That's interesting, Ice 6 is being avoided in my games because we find being at 0 cards too dangerous at almost any time that's not the start of the game (edited)
RepoRogue 23/02/2026 16:14
Sure, but if you play it into a line where it brings you above the compile threshold, its generally either going to get flipped immediately or go away.
Eris 23/02/2026 16:20
True, but you can only play it in that scenario
16:21
Not necessarily saying Love 6 is really good, but something good it has compared to a few 6s is that you can use it as a big point advantage for control

18:34

RepoRogue 23/02/2026 18:47
I'm pretty high on Fire and Plague. I think Love can feel really bad to play against, but my instinct is that the feelsbad is bigger than the actual average impact. In Love's defense, I think the typical card game logic of milling good cards not mattering because it could have been on the bottom of your deck doesn't really apply when you are drawing through your deck, sometimes multiple times per game.
TMind
It's especially the random draw from his hand that crippled his deck the most
RepoRogue 23/02/2026 18:49
How much better was the random draw than Fear's random discard? My instinct is that the random discard is usually better since you're not giving your opponent another card (which dilutes the effectiveness of your other discard effects).
18:51

10:34

10:43

10:43

Sean 27/02/2026 9:34
New player here from London here, howdy! I’ve read all comments including the designers on Psy 1 but as former competitive mtg player isn’t Psy 1 meta warping. Ie when drafting if it’s available then you pick it hoping to draft the support cards, which forces the opponent to draft either the counters or the support cards for it. I guess what I’m asking is why would someone not draft Psy 1 and go for a combo every time it’s possible? For example is Psy 1 and its support cards generally a weaker protocol? What are the tournament rules?

Sean
New player here from London here, howdy! I’ve read all comments including the designers on Psy 1 but as former competitive mtg player isn’t Psy 1 meta warping. Ie when drafting if it’s available then you pick it hoping to draft the support cards, which forces the opponent to draft either the counters or the support cards for it. dgbm 27/02/2026 11:29
It's a clunky, predictable combo that can be countered in a number of ways (including via the Control component); to reverse your question, why would anyone forfeit drafting various aggro, control, and other combo strategies, or one of a wide variety of hybrid builds that they might find more enjoyable and be more proficient in playing, in order to just constantly go for the same combo?
11:36
Also, unless I'm forgetting something, the combo requires Psychic and either Speed, Darkness or Chaos; I don't have that much playtime with Darkness, but Chaos and Speed are among the more popular protocols in my group, due both to power level and fun factor, and both get picked very often (also because they are fantastic support protocols), which serves as a natural balancing aspect (obviosuly anecdotal).
Sean
New player here from London here, howdy! I’ve read all comments including the designers on Psy 1 but as former competitive mtg player isn’t Psy 1 meta warping. I guess what I’m asking is why would someone not draft Psy 1 and go for a combo every time it’s possible? For example is Psy 1 and its support cards generally a weaker protocol? Eris 27/02/2026 12:04
I don't think we can see yet the impact it'll have in the meta but I've been playing against it a bit lately and what I can say is: All more "competitive" games I've had or seen have been with Main/Aux1, this includes one and a half small tournaments. There's someone who has been doing really well in them who is playing Speed+Psy, but the Psy1 combo hasn't come up in the games I know of (which aren't all of them). He's just really good. Outside of that person, I don't believe Psychic has been played/banned much. These are not a lot of games so that's just the trend right now here. In the games I've been playing against Psy 1 it only happened once and the player lost, so the combo hasn't been that relevant.

fruit 27/02/2026 12:08
I agree with the others on Psy 1 or Psy in general. Since you are locked in to draft 6 cards at once, going for a single combo is not consistent enough to win tournaments. To make it meta warping you would need more draw or tutoring and something to move around opposite face down cards in case you play it too late and a forced face-down compile becomes unstoppable
1
Eris 27/02/2026 12:09
About the strategy itself, the problem, as Dgbm says, is that if you go for it it locks your draft, and I'm not sure it's always strong enough to justify it. It does give you at least a few turns advantage, so depending of the time it happens it can be really game defining, but if you are being ran over the rest of the game it won't be enough.

Eris 27/02/2026 12:15
I think Speed+Psy+something else is your better option, but the combo itself is easier to disrupt and more costly. If you opponent saves a card for when you use Speed 3, bye and you also lost Speed 3's bottom command. If they have control, they refresh and then bye. If they have a deck that can keep a lot of points in your Psy line, you'll only get a couple turns of advantage. And also you have to hold Speed 3, which is a key card, in your hand waiting for Psy 1.
12:16
So if you are lucky and draw the cards at the right time it will be really good, but it doesn't seem to be super consistent

Michael Yang 28/02/2026 0:57

TheBigBoy 28/02/2026 1:45
They could, but the concern around the combo is that drafting around it becomes too important, and the presence of counters doesn't really fix that problem
1:46
although it does help a little
1:47
generally as cardpools get bigger, the ways to deal with the strongest stuff get outpaced by the ways to do the strongest stuff, so it's important that whatever ends up being the strongest stuff is fun (which psy 1 combos are not )
1:51
Admittedly my worries are pretty narrow, because they concern only a certain kind of player(base): Those who are tryharding when they play, but not necessarily in a competitive environment like a tournament People who are more casual will just not try to abuse the combo because it's not fun, and on the flip side in a tournament everyone accepts that it's about winning primarily so don't begrudge getting combo-d on. So it's really only a worry for the niche type of players I'm used to, where we're too competitive to just not take a best thing just because it's unfun, and not competitive enough to enjoy the game if it's just about cutthroat MtG-style threat vs answer. Players like us need the house-errata Sorry for the thoughtdump (edited)
1
Impalsi 28/02/2026 2:43
I'm going to try a "What's the play?" from my tournament finals game with Makario. Though it wasn't the flashiest play, I thought this was a really neat spot. (edited)
2:44
Makario has 4 cards in deck, 6 in hand, 3 in discard (Light 5, Hate 5, Spirit 5). We know Makario has Hate 3 in hand. I have Water 5, Speed 1. My facedown cards (from left to right) are Water 0, Life 3, and Life 4 And though its order is unknown, my deck is known to contain Water 2, Speed 5, and Life 5 My trash contains: Life 0, Water 1, Water 3, Water 4 Our possible plays are: Water 5 Speed 1 Play something facedown Refresh (spending control) What do you do and why? To help get you plugged into the context, I'll point out that Makario has a very powerful interaction engine set up. Consider how that interacts with our board. (edited)
2:44
1
2:47
If people like this "What's the play?", I'd love to do more! Please let me know if you have any feedback on the format.
TheBigBoy 28/02/2026 6:04
Interesting spot since at first it looks like things are dire, but then it seems they’re better than they look because you’re so ahead on value. I think i’m going water 5 here. If it’s not answered you get to clear that spirit 1 and hate 0 off the board which helps a lot. The 2 turns of tempo loss should be ok being up so much value and a compile. If it’s answered then you still get a way better refresh. Playing speed 1 or refreshing now both seem like your position isn’t much better next turn. (edited)

Sean 02/03/2026 11:01
I like your take and it’s certainly in the spirit of playing games for fun. Where we might disagree is that blaming the players for wanting to win when the game system allows for it is somehow the player’s fault and not the games fault. This sort of game is by design confrontational and deterministic, which therefore owes it to the players to have inherent balance. I’m not experienced enough to know the all of the outs to the combo, but the point is that two players who want to win are forced to study a single combo line first (by design) which is not fun for either player as it has gated their exploration of a system (ie explore at will but first do your homework that this quirk exists). I hope that makes sense. Would be great to hear @Michael Yang’s perspective. (Edit: typo) (edited)
1
Philtastic 02/03/2026 18:42
Anyone got some recommended protocol combos to give to first time players? Letting them randomly select based on themes often ends with very unsynergetic decks.
henri 02/03/2026 18:58
Life Water Speed was what I played my first game. Themes sounded like they'd go well with one anotherand they were a simple intro with synergies and not too much remove/discard.
makario
I'd recommend Light, Spirit, Speed as first 3 because it teaches "flow" quite well.
  • What's the difference between top, middle, and bottom commands? Light has a bottom command. Spirit and Speed have top commands.
  • How do triggers work? Light has an "End" trigger, Spirit a "Start" trigger and a "Draw" trigger, Speed a "clear cache" trigger.
  • How do shifts and flips work? Plenty of cards in this protocol set to practice with Some questions that will likely come out of this set that are good thought exercises:
  • Is Speed 0 any good? I don't get why this matters
  • How do I best capitalize on Spirit 1, given that I have to discard at the start of every turn?
  • How many cards do I draw if I flip this card with Light 0?
 (edited)
Impalsi 02/03/2026 19:01
makario had this excellent post on the topic @Philtastic
2
henri 02/03/2026 19:03
I recommend not touching psyche, plague, hate, or love for your first games (to either player).
Eris 02/03/2026 19:05
Except if the new player likes to be mean
19:05
Then Psyche and Plague are good
Impalsi 02/03/2026 19:05
Psychic 2 is insanely overpowered if you're not playing with control
19:05
So yeah if the new player would get a kick out of wasting your compiles, that could be fun for them : )
Eris 02/03/2026 19:15
Aside from Makario's great answer, some combinations that can be good when you want to start exploring some options:
  • Life+Water: really easy, lets you play lots of face down cards and interact with them, Light/Darkness/Speed are good third options
  • Fire+Light: Fire can be a bit difficult to manage at first because you are discarding your own cards, Light has a lot of drawing and makes it easier. Good to constantly target your opponent's cards
  • Speed+Gravity: this one gets more complicated but can bring a lot of the moments that discovering this game has. The idea is to use Gravity cards to retrigger Speed 0 as much as you can.
  • Plague+Psychic: mentioned above, good if the person likes to be mean and loose their friends

Sean
I like your take and it’s certainly in the spirit of playing games for fun. Where we might disagree is that blaming the players for wanting to win when the game system allows for it is somehow the player’s fault and not the games fault. This sort of game is by design confrontational and deterministic, which therefore owes it to the players to have inherent balance. I’m not experienced enough to know the all of the outs to the combo, but the point is that two players who want to win are forced to study a single combo line first (by design) which is not fun for either player as it has gated their exploration of a system (ie explore at will but first do your homework that this quirk exists). I hope that makes sense. Would be great to hear @Michael Yang’s perspective. (Edit: typo) (edited)
Michael Yang 02/03/2026 23:11
I'm not sure what to say - I could write a dissertation on this topic, learn more in the process, and have to write a new one. There's a lot at play here: human behavior, the realities of game development, the joys of games in general, etc. I'm happy to answer more specific questions if there are any.
Michael Yang
I'm not sure what to say - I could write a dissertation on this topic, learn more in the process, and have to write a new one. There's a lot at play here: human behavior, the realities of game development, the joys of games in general, etc. I'm happy to answer more specific questions if there are any.
Sean 02/03/2026 23:36
You’ve created something fun and memorable, so there’s lots to be proud of here. My comment was not intended as a criticism, just a reality of a portion of the client base for any technical card game. I guess my specific question is about your thought processes on the Psy1 combo interactions with multiple protocols and why they were designed that way, or whether you would create tournament rules. You’ve called it a fair (but strong) combo interaction (to paraphrase) but I was just curious about the design process behind that. I’m asking to learn, not to criticise (as I have been more of a technical card gamer player rather than a designer). Wishing you all the best of course and look forward to main 3!
Michael Yang 03/03/2026 1:32
Criticism is necessary and encouraged! Something important to keep in mind: 4 years ago when Psychic 1 was developed, I was the best Compile player in the world by a longshot. Today, I don't think I crack the top 50. As players learn, I have to learn. And as players give feedback, I have to listen. The most important question was always and will always be "Would someone be excited to play this?"
TheBigBoy 03/03/2026 1:43
Psy 1 is an exciting card without the combos i think
1:43
There’s nothing else really like it
1:43
Where it can potentially take you to the winstate in an unstoppable way
1:44
It’s similar the fantasy of metal 1 but way more likely to actually happen
1:44
Where even if the card is a little weak for 95% of the game, in the clutch it does something no other card does (well, less true now with the alt-compile protocols)
Michael Yang 03/03/2026 2:13
I still enjoy trying to pull off the Unity compile cheat but have only ever done it once. If the data shows that it's underpowered, I'd likely design a set of protocols that focus on giving it more opportunities to achieve its power fantasy. Since the beginning, it was always my intent to run "seasons" for Compile where there'd be sets of protocols regularly designated for league play.
TheBigBoy 03/03/2026 2:25
I think it is weakish but that’s healthy and I assumed intentional
2:26

2:27

I still enjoy trying to pull off the Unity compile cheat but have only ever done it once. Eris 03/03/2026 8:51
I've only played a game with Unity and I felt the design was really really cool. I have no idea if the protocol needs more power, but I think the fact that the compile is even possible is in itself a powerful effect to be taken into account. It imposes a limitation for your opponent, forcing them to calculate how possible it is for it to happen and spend resources on cards that aren't itself so dangerous while fighting for control.
8:54
And Unity 1 felt to me like it is a good card even without the middle text

BlueMoons 03/03/2026 9:28
Waterfall 0 // Start: if this card is covered, return it to your hand. // Choose a protocol. You Scan 4. If the match's value is lower than a covered card in this stack, you may play it. Otherwise, draw 1. // -- Where Scan N would be a keyword for 'reveal the top card of your deck. If there was a choice and the card matches the choice (protocol, value, command, trigger type) end the Scan and this card is considered the match. Put the top card of your deck on the bottom. Do this a maximum of N times.' (edited)

9:32

12:01
"subtract" - every x effect text that contains a number subtracts 1 from that number. So the draw from darkness 0 would jump from 3 to 2
12:03
"impulsive play" - powerful play effect that lets you play cards, however the cards get deleted after a round
12:05
"neighbour"- refers to the card directly neighboring this card (example this card is the 3rd card in your middle stack so it refers to the 3rd cards in the left and right stacks)
12:09
I think neighbour is the best idea
12:11
Bottom effect: "End: Flip your covered or uncovered face down neighbours"

NotWhale 04/03/2026 9:02
I've been thinking about it and it breaks the game a little
NotWhale 04/03/2026 9:13
According to @Impalsi's supplement rules, shuffling a 2-card deck is a gamestate-changing action But with such an effect we could derive from seeing the top card whether the order of the cards has changed So if only you see the top card we need to figure out what to make of shuffling a two-card deck
9:15
Both seeing the top card is also weird Revealing in this game is defined and is a momentary action So in order for the wording to make sense it would need to change the rules of the game, like 'the top card of your deck is a public information' or smth
9:16
Not not doable, but there's surprisingly much stuff going on with 'play with the card revealed'-type effects
Funmaker 04/03/2026 9:23
“You may look at the top card at any time” doesn’t reveal it to the opponent. It just lets you look at it. I also don’t really see the problem with shuffle triggers. If the effect is “after you shuffle, draw 1” you can also immediately tell if the game state has changed.
Funmaker
“You may look at the top card at any time” doesn’t reveal it to the opponent. It just lets you look at it. I also don’t really see the problem with shuffle triggers. If the effect is “after you shuffle, draw 1” you can also immediately tell if the game state has changed.
NotWhale 04/03/2026 9:44
Exactly, but your opponent cannot
9:45
Which means that from their perspective it has changed, but from yours it hasn't (edited)
Funmaker 04/03/2026 11:34
I think that argument doesn’t make sense, it could always not change.
NotWhale
According to @Impalsi's supplement rules, shuffling a 2-card deck is a gamestate-changing action But with such an effect we could derive from seeing the top card whether the order of the cards has changed So if only you see the top card we need to figure out what to make of shuffling a two-card deck
BlueMoons 04/03/2026 15:22
since shuffles are non-deterministic in cases with two or more cards, imo shuffle triggers go when the gamestate could have changed, not whether they actually did or not
BlueMoons
since shuffles are non-deterministic in cases with two or more cards, imo shuffle triggers go when the gamestate could have changed, not whether they actually did or not

RepoRogue 10/03/2026 17:37
There are also just a ton of cool little combos you can do. If you can get Fire 0 in your Water line, you have a repeatable draw 3, flip 2 effect with Water 4. Fire 3 can flip your Speed 3 back up, trigger another round of shifts and also letting you flip it back during a future end of turn window (this might be even better than I think it is if you can trigger Speed 3 in the same End phase as you flip it with Fire 3). I was overall really impressed by how smoothly it all played together! (edited)
fruit 10/03/2026 18:48
I made use of mirror today and won the match! I last picked mirror Darkness, Water, Mirror Psychic, Gravity, Fire I had the notion that mirror would be good against gravity because I could follow up the attempts to control my stacks and protocols (from both psychic and grav). Water 2 helped as well. Mirror 1 drew a lot of attention from fire which actually helped me as well because it meant that they weren't able to pull off combos and had to delay a lot. In the end I won 3-2

Coffee 12/03/2026 22:23

fruit
how would you take notes during a competitive game?
Impalsi 12/03/2026 22:24
pen and paper?
henri 12/03/2026 23:16
If you refer to notation as in chess, both players documenting each action, michael shared his favorite text way to denote boardstates and actions a while back.
JoeKundlak 13/03/2026 0:17
Forwarded
I really love this idea. I use this if anyone wants to make something of it: (0: H2, 1: H3, H0, fd) (3: Pl1, 5: Pl4) I usually write notes to myself with something like this. Each stack is numbered: 0 is Player 1's left stack, 2 is their right stack, 3 is the stack opposite 0, and 5 is the stack opposite 2. If the stack ID is in the list, then we know it has cards in it. Cards are ordered in a stack from first to last; the last card being the uncovered card in the stack. In this example, H = Hate, Pl = Plague, and fd = face-down card.
Originally sent: 16/10/2025 22:33
0:17
This you mean?
fruit 13/03/2026 0:21
thanks
0:22
although I didn't mean record notation like in chess (this is still useful)
henri 13/03/2026 0:40
I sometimes scribble myself a checklist of stuff I need to watch in regards to the opponents protocols, or take notes for handreading. Sometimes to calculate odds. I don't want to bring any prep that isn't in my head to a table, neither do I want to leave open whether im using external tools, hence if I ever rarely bring any help for myself, it's a piece of paper scrap and a ballpoint.

Michael Yang 13/03/2026 3:35
I believe in MTG Arena they let you see the cards that you "know". Like if a card is returned to your opponent's hand, you'd be able to see it until they take some action that would obfuscate that information. Something like that could be fun in Compile Digital. There are a lot less things to track in Compile though so not sure how it actually plays out.

TheBigBoy 13/03/2026 3:38

3:38
like if someone Time 3's something it would be nice to not have to remember forever what it is
1
henri 13/03/2026 5:27
Personally, I believe that maintaining memory of your opponent's hand is a skill which I dislike to see made obsolete. I want to be rewarded for tracking information. I've also come around to value the skill of how quickly you can read out information that is open to you, or even doing it in advance before it influences your decision. Could we just reveal the information I could have deduced if I had memorized all I had seen and could count out based on what I see now? Sure we could, but it would devalue the time advantage I'd have earned over my opponent if I counted out or tracked that information in advance and my opponent didn't. I also might gain information based on other information which I might not have anymore. I also can no longer make mistakes I might have made if the game didn't do the work for me.
1
5:27
I think it's a preference of whether you like an automated system partaking in the game or not. How close to watching two computers play each other do you want to be? Different people enjoy different amounts of cognitive engagement. Some want to relax and watch the game cascade like a movie plot. I tend to enjoy games most, when I can feel my brain reaching capacity. I want to keep track of all the information manually and be rewarded, if my opponent is lazier or less focussed. I want to have to make the decision of to which parts of the game I allocate my limited time and limited thought. That is a core and fun skill to me. When I play compile, I did not pick up the box with the intent to throw cards from my hand and laugh about funny cascades unfolding which I did not foresee. Very likely someone enjoys exactly that about compile. And that is equally valid. Automation is without question great, when it does not change any information gained or decisions to be made. There just has to be a conscious choice about whether a digital version of compile is an app to play compile, or a digital alternate version of compile. If for example Reveal is permanent, that changes how the keyword functions and how the game plays in ways I mention above. Even small things, like being able to track which cards my opponent has held for how long introduce new information that can matter massively.
2

rfoconnor 13/03/2026 14:08
because you'll need to play a turn in between each. Yes, you lock your opponent into a refresh each turn you trigger Mirror 1's End effect and point it at Peace 1, but to get to your End step, you'll have to compile, play a card, or refresh. So you can't comb through your deck to find your favorite five cards.
rfoconnor
because you'll need to play a turn in between each. Yes, you lock your opponent into a refresh each turn you trigger Mirror 1's End effect and point it at Peace 1, but to get to your End step, you'll have to compile, play a card, or refresh. So you can't comb through your deck to find your favorite five cards.
Coffee 13/03/2026 14:11
but both players discards their hand with peace 1, so your opponent is forced to refresh, but so are you, and that refresh gets you to the end step, and it doesn't change the state of the field since both players refreshed, and you could do that forever until you drew your favourite cards

loganmcbroom 13/03/2026 18:39
Mtg has some rulings to handle this exact sort of problem. There's an old combo deck called 'four horseman' that mills itself and needs the cards to come out a certain way which usually happens within a few tries. It's banned in tournaments by a general "loops must forward game state" rule, but also because, even though the probability of success approaches 1 in the limit, that does not mean it will happen in a finite amount of time.

Followup: time assim smoke won 3/4 vs life death grav. Smoke really didn’t do much (several good smoke 1 plays and 1 gigantic smoke 2 vs grav 0, but otherwise kinda blank cards), but assim was insane.
20:21
Secret power of assim is having a 6 and basically 2 4’s (since the 2 acts like a 4). Really efficient
20:23
Interesting quirk about assim 4 and 6, is that as you play the game your good cards are often on board, while your less good cards are in the trash and then deck (discarded to 5’s or in this case time 4) (edited)
20:23
So you more often than not have good odds to give away less good cards
20:24
Whereas assim 2 you slam early and have better odds to take good stuff
20:25
But the main really good opening play was assim 2-> smoke 1 to trigger the stolen card and then get another assim 2 trigger

Hello, welcome! I have played a bunch of games where I selected protocols entirely devoted to comboing Psy 1 I'm not at all convinced that it's a good strategy, much less a broken or meta warping one It is very shocking when you discover that it's possible and feels extremely oppressive when it happens, but it's very telegraphed, inconsistent, and capable opponents can see it coming and set themselves up to not be destroyed by it

RepoRogue 17/03/2026 15:59
To be clear, I don't think Gravity is uncounterable by any means. My argument for it being the optimal first pick is instead based on how consistent and stable it is. By giving you a bit of everything, it asks nothing from you in terms of drafting and doesn't give your opponent anything obvious to counter draft. But this was also very much an attempt to start s conversation, not a strongly held opinion. I personally have been prioritizing Speed in my drafts and have had a lot of success with it.

RepoRogue 17/03/2026 19:42

RepoRogue 18/03/2026 1:02
I think Speed 0 is in the top tier, personally. It's definitely narrower than the other cards up there, but the potential is just so high that its worth doing some inefficient stuff to get there. Since I like playing Speed combo so much, I think I generally rate unconditional card draw higher than you. Darkness 0 and Speed 1 are generally draw 3 even if you have a full hand, which has a lot of value in terms of finding combo pieces. Although I do agree that those cards are generally weak. Light 0 is hilariously better than both in terms of its potential to draw you cards and because it has so much flexibility.

nd 23/03/2026 22:53
just a quick rules question: if one has the control, we're allowed to switch protocols before compiling right? We're playing it like this but in some situations it feels pretty op so I wanted to clarify this within my playgroup.
Eris
I imagine it can be overwhelming to start with the 30 protocols
nd 23/03/2026 22:54
Can absolutely agree on this, we're currently dizzy by choice but decided to pick random ones in order to learn the game haha.
nd
just a quick rules question: if one has the control, we're allowed to switch protocols before compiling right? We're playing it like this but in some situations it feels pretty op so I wanted to clarify this within my playgroup.
Michael Yang 23/03/2026 22:57
This is correct. The first thing you do when you refresh or compile is use control if you have it. It's a very important mechanic strategically.
Michael Yang
This is correct. The first thing you do when you refresh or compile is use control if you have it. It's a very important mechanic strategically.
nd 23/03/2026 22:59
Thanks for the quick clarification! So control becomes even more important for us (as a playgroup). Love to see that, bc it diversifies the strategy and mandates planning around control as a center component imo.
1
Michael Yang 23/03/2026 23:01
Definitely! It also makes the different phases of the game feel very different. Early game, control is for setting up combos and your late game. Midgame, you don't need it, but you better know how you're going to get it. Late game, you have to fight hard for it or at least get by with keeping it neutral.
Michael Yang
Definitely! It also makes the different phases of the game feel very different. Early game, control is for setting up combos and your late game. Midgame, you don't need it, but you better know how you're going to get it. Late game, you have to fight hard for it or at least get by with keeping it neutral.
nd 23/03/2026 23:07
Haha, you definetly got a read on (new?) players there bc this is what I basically do in my games so far. Feels really bad to have to fight for control when you're behind, so my gameplan typically is getting it early and maintaining it; unless I have to sacrifice it midgame for short term value. So props for game design!
23:08
(making this topic [getting // maintaining control] feeling intuitive is pretty difficult I assume)
Funny Samy 23/03/2026 23:08

Coffee 28/03/2026 15:16
What does Apathy play well with? genuine question, I have not played enough Apathy to get a feel for it
TheBigBoy 28/03/2026 15:31
Apathy is not a great protocol overall, but Death helps (edited)
Coffee
What does Apathy play well with? genuine question, I have not played enough Apathy to get a feel for it
Eris 28/03/2026 16:09
Apathy is kind of weird because it looks like a face down protocol but doesn't really have a lot of synergies with face down interaction, mainly because the cards are flipped face down once they are covered (so no that much retriggering with flips or effects that shift face down cards). That said I've tried specifically Smoke and it does well, because Smoke mostly needs consistent ways to make sure you have face down cards in your lines. Apathy also has the opponent control Smoke lacks
Eris
Apathy is kind of weird because it looks like a face down protocol but doesn't really have a lot of synergies with face down interaction, mainly because the cards are flipped face down once they are covered (so no that much retriggering with flips or effects that shift face down cards). That said I've tried specifically Smoke and it does well, because Smoke mostly needs consistent ways to make sure you have face down cards in your lines. Apathy also has the opponent control Smoke lacks
